title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
code: public static int CharacterReplacement(string s, int k)

frames.caption: Set up an empty window [left, right], a freq map of char counts inside it, and k = 1 (max replacements). Walk right across the string once.
frames.note: A window is valid when, after replacing at most k of its chars, every char is equal. The non-dominant chars are precisely the ones we replace, so validity is (windowLen − maxFreq) ≤ k. We track maxFreq (the top single-char count) to make that test O(1).

frames.caption: right=0: admit 'A'. freq['A'] = 1.
frames.note: This is the LONGEST variable-window template: grow `right` unconditionally, then test validity. The validity test is the whole problem restated — (windowLen − maxFreq) is exactly how many chars differ from the most frequent one, i.e. how many you would have to replace. If that count is ≤ k the window is achievable, so we keep it.

frames.caption: maxFreq = max(maxFreq, freq['A']=1) = 1.
frames.note: maxFreq is the highest single-char count anywhere in the window. We only ever raise it (a high-water mark) — never lower it on a shrink. That sounds wrong, but it is the famous LC 424 subtlety: a stale-high maxFreq just makes the window slide instead of shrink, and the window can only re-expand once a real char count actually catches up, so we never report a width wider than a genuinely valid one.

frames.caption: Window "A" len 1: needReplace = 1 − 1 = 0 ≤ k (1). Valid — no shrink.
frames.note: This is the LONGEST variable-window template: grow `right` unconditionally, then test validity. The validity test is the whole problem restated — (windowLen − maxFreq) is exactly how many chars differ from the most frequent one, i.e. how many you would have to replace. If that count is ≤ k the window is achievable, so we keep it.

frames.caption: best = max(best, 1) = 1. Widest valid window so far: "A".
frames.note: After validity is restored we record the width right − left + 1 as a running maximum. The answer is the widest validly-reached window — replace its (len − maxFreq) ≤ k minority chars and the window becomes uniform. Space is O(1): the count map holds at most 26 uppercase letters.

frames.caption: right=1: admit 'A'. freq['A'] = 2.

frames.caption: maxFreq = max(maxFreq, freq['B']=2) = 3.

frames.caption: Window "AABAB" len 5: needReplace = 5 − 3 = 2 > k (1). Invalid — shrink.
frames.note: The window is invalid: we would need to replace more than k chars to make it uniform. Because maxFreq is frozen, we drop exactly ONE char on the left per `right`. The window size therefore never decreases — it slides forward by one. left and right each only move forward, bounded by n, so the whole scan is O(n).

frames.caption: Drop 'A' on the left, left → 1. Window slides to "ABAB", len 4 (needReplace 1).
frames.note: The window is invalid: we would need to replace more than k chars to make it uniform. Because maxFreq is frozen, we drop exactly ONE char on the left per `right`. The window size therefore never decreases — it slides forward by one. left and right each only move forward, bounded by n, so the whole scan is O(n).

frames.caption: best = max(best, 4) = 4. Widest valid window so far: "ABAB".
frames.note: After validity is restored we record the width right − left + 1 as a running maximum. The answer is the widest validly-reached window — replace its (len − maxFreq) ≤ k minority chars and the window becomes uniform. Space is O(1): the count map holds at most 26 uppercase letters.

frames.caption: End of string. Longest window needing ≤ 1 replacement is length 4 (e.g. "AABA" → replace one B-spot to get "AAAA").
frames.note: Both pointers only move forward, so the scan is O(n); the count map is O(1) (≤ 26 letters). The answer is the widest validly-reached width — not the final window — because best is tracked as a running maximum, and the frozen-maxFreq sliding guarantees the window never narrows below it.
